macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Core Question: Can New Growth Negate a Forbidden Core?

micro_segments.title: Setup: The Problem of Growth from Forbidden Sources (Orlah, Kilayim, Terumah, Shemittah)
The Ran's Alternative Framing: Status Transfer vs. Bittul
The Amoraic Dispute: Rabbi Yitzchak (Permissible) vs. Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Yonatan (Forbidden)

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Principle: Davar Sheyesh Lo Matirin is Never Batel

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: Things with a Path to Permissibility (Tevel, Maaser Sheni, Hekdesh, Chadash)
Explanation: Why These Items Cannot Be Nullified—The Alternative Path Principle
Contrast: Davar She'ein Lo Matirin (Terumah, Orlah, Kilayim) Requires an Amount Threshold
The Challenge from Shevi'it: A Case Without Matirin That Is Still Not Batel
Rabbi Shimon's Response: Shevi'it is Special Regarding Bi'ur, Not Eating
Rishonim's Reinterpretation: Eating Shevi'it as Yesh Lo Matirin (Flexibility Within Status)

macro_segments.title: Testing the Negation Question with Onions: The Mishna of Betzalim Sheyardu Aleihem Geshamim

micro_segments.title: Mishna Setup: Onions That Grew Across the Shemittah Transition (Black vs. Green Leaves)
Rabbi Chanina ben Antignos's Alternative: Leaf Strength as Indicator of New Growth
Apparent Proof: 'Gidulei Heter Ma'alin Et Ha'Issur'—New Growth Seems to Negate the Core
First Gemara Evasion: Maybe It's Ground Up (Miduchanim), Not Whole

macro_segments.title: The Baraita of Weeding with a Samaritan: Evidence That New Growth Permits the Mixture

micro_segments.title: Setup: Working in a Field Where the Core May Be from Shemittah, Eating Fresh Vegetables
Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar: Permission After Shemittah Regardless of Shemittah Suspicion
Apparent Conclusion: 'Gidulei Heter Ma'alin Et Ha'Issur'—Growth Negates the Core
First Objection: Maybe It's Wheat (Zaro Kalah), Not Onions (Ayyin Zaro Kalah)
Gemara Ruling: The Baraita Explicitly Names Vegetables (Chasayos—Leeks, Garlic, Onions)
Second Objection: Maybe the Mixture Is Ground Up (Miduchanim)
Refutation: The Baraita Specifies Direct Weeding (Hamenakesh), Not Processed Food

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak's Resolution: Shemittah is Unique Because It Focuses on the Land

micro_segments.title: The Answer: 'Shanya Shemittah'—Its Prohibition Comes from the Land, So Its Nullification Does Too
Conceptual Explanation: Shemittah Asks 'Which Year Is This From?' Not 'How Much Grew?'
Tosafos's Alternative: Active vs. Passive—Only Natural Growth, Not Deliberate Permitting, Counts

macro_segments.title: Counterevidence from Maaser: Why the Original Portion Is Not Negated

micro_segments.title: Question: If Shemittah Growth Negates the Core, Why Doesn't Maaser Growth?
Baraita: A Liter of Tevel Maaser Grain is Planted and Grows to Ten Liters
The Law: New Growth Requires Maaser and Shevi'it Obligations, But the Original One Liter Remains Problematic
Gemara's Resolution: Maaser Focuses on Processing (Digun), Not Land; Therefore Original Core Never Negated

macro_segments.title: Synthesis: The General Rule and the Exception

micro_segments.title: Conclusion: Growth Does Not Negate the Core in General; Shemittah is an Exception
Why Shemittah Differs: It Asks a Global Question About the Produce's Origin Year
Why Other Cases Differ: They Require Human Processing Components and Focus on the Object, Not the Land

topical_tags.term: Davar Sheyesh Lo Matirin (Thing with a path to permissibility)
Davar She'ein Lo Matirin (Thing without a path to permissibility)
Notein Ta'am (Imparting flavor, standard bittul ratio)
Ein Mevatlin Issur Le'Chatchila (Cannot intentionally nullify forbidden items)
Rabbi Yitzchak (Amora, permissive position on growth)
Rabbi Yochanan (Amora, restrictive position on growth)
Rabbi Yonatan (Amora, restrictive position on growth)
Rabbi Shimon (Tanna, originator of Davar Sheyesh Lo Matirin principle)
Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar (Tanna, Samaritan weeding baraita)
Rabbi Chanina ben Antignos (Tanna, leaf strength test for growth)
Ran (Rishon, reinterprets Shimon's position on Shvi'it)
Ayyin Zaro Kalah (Seed remains—vegetable type like onions)
